topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay spread across the kitchen table, the stark lettering of the genetic report swimming before my eyes. My hand, trembling slightly, hovered over the page, tracing the lines that detailed parentage. Every word seemed to vibrate with an impossible reality. I reread the section detailing the match percentage, the statistical probability, and then the confirmation. A slow, cold wave washed over me, leaving a hollow ache in my chest. I pushed the paper away, the sound of it rustling loud in the silence. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing air that suddenly felt too thin. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid, as I stared blankly at the framed photograph on the wall—a picture of a family I’d always assumed was just… a family.

[story 2]

I sat on the edge of my bed, the plastic evidence resting on my lap. The numbers blurred. My mind refused to process the sheer weight of what the results implied. I kept blinking, trying to force the information into a logical framework, but the pieces wouldn't connect. My fingers drummed a frantic rhythm against my thigh. When I finally looked up, my gaze drifted to the ceiling, unfocused. A strange dryness coated my throat. I whispered the names listed on the report, each syllable sounding alien and profoundly misplaced. I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, squeezing them tight, trying to physically hold the vision back. A low groan escaped my lips, a sound of utter disbelief wrestling with a sudden, terrifying sense of belonging.

[story 3]

The fluorescent light in the office seemed too bright, exposing every minute detail of the DNA results. I stared at the screen, my jaw slackening slightly as I absorbed the data. It wasn't just information; it was a fundamental restructuring of my entire existence. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under my weight, and slowly pushed my chair back, the scrape echoing loudly. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the knuckles turning white. I took a deep, shuddering breath, trying to anchor myself to the solid reality of the room, but the air felt thick, viscous. I looked down at my own hands, then back at the screen, my eyes wide and unblinking, unable to form a coherent thought or movement.
